one_line_takeaway: WAPIIBench shows open-source code LLMs correctly generate compliant web API (Axios/JavaScript) invocations well under half the time -- with GPT-4o clearly ahead at 60-77% -- and frequently hallucinate nonexistent endpoints and arguments; this is a correctness/hallucination benchmarking paper, not a security paper.
key_finding: No open-source model solved more than 40% of tasks (Code Llama 70B: 30% correct implementations on full completion, 40% on argument completion), while GPT-4o reached 60% (full completion) and 77% (argument completion), the best of any evaluated model. Across models, hallucination is substantial: up to 39% of URLs and up to 31% of arguments generated were illegal (i.e., not defined by the API specification at all). Models predict HTTP methods comparatively well (mostly >80% correct (e)) but struggle much more with URLs (correct rate 32%-72% (e)) and argument names (precision 51%-75%, recall 33%-69% (e)); when an argument name was correctly identified, its value was correct 53%-84% of the time (e), suggesting endpoint/argument selection -- not value assignment -- is the harder subtask. Larger model size within a family did not always help (DeepSeek-Coder, Qwen2.5-Coder, and Code Llama families each had a mid-sized variant underperform both smaller and larger siblings), and models with near-zero full-completion correctness (Qwen2.5-Coder, Llama 3.1) largely failed to produce executable code at all (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder often refused to continue the starter code).
core_contribution: The paper's actual PDF content (title: 'Benchmarking Web API Integration Code Generation', introducing 'WAPIIBench') is a benchmark and evaluation-only study, not a mitigation approach. Its own contributions are: (1) WAPIIBench, a first-of-its-kind dataset of 395 (task, expected request-configuration) triples covering all endpoints of four real-world OpenAPI-specified web APIs (Asana, Google Calendar, Google Sheets, Slack Web); (2) an open-source, execution-based evaluation pipeline that safely intercepts generated Axios/JavaScript API calls via a mock execution environment ('Mock') and compares the captured request configuration against ground truth and the API specification using fine-grained metrics (correct/illegal URLs, methods, argument precision/recall, argument value conditional accuracy); and (3) an empirical study of 21 open-source code LLMs (CodeT5+, StarCoder, StarCoder2, DeepSeek-Coder, DeepSeek-Coder-V2, Qwen2.5-Coder, Code Llama, Llama 3.1) plus GPT-4o and GPT-4o mini as a commercial upper bound, measuring how correctly each generates web API invocation code in two setups (full completion: model must find the endpoint; argument completion: endpoint given, model must fill correct arguments).
threat_model: N/A -- this is a code-correctness/hallucination benchmark, not a security study. There is no adversary, attack, or vulnerability class defined anywhere in the paper's own methodology; 'illegal' invocations mean specification-noncompliant (e.g., hallucinated endpoint/argument), not malicious. Do not conflate with security-flavored citations used only as motivating context in the introduction (e.g., prior work on Copilot code security, insecure AI-assisted code) -- those are related work, not this paper's contribution.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the dataset simplifies real-world integration code (limited surrounding program context, and only outgoing-request correctness is evaluated, not response handling); (2) benchmark quality is bounded by the quality/completeness of the underlying OpenAPI specifications, some of which had to be manually fixed, and free-form textual usage constraints in parameter descriptions are not captured by the automated correctness analysis; (3) despite manual vetting, synthetic tasks generated by Gemini 1.5 Pro tend to underuse optional parameters and use placeholder rather than realistic example values, limiting transferability to real-world tasks; results are also limited to 4 APIs and a single fixed prompt (no per-model prompt engineering), so performance for any given model could likely be improved and may not generalize to other APIs.

datasets_used: WAPIIBench (this paper's own dataset): 395 task/request-configuration pairs, one per endpoint, across 4 real-world OpenAPI-specified APIs -- Asana (167 endpoints: 61 POST/79 GET/14 PUT/13 DELETE/0 PATCH), Google Calendar (37: 14/11/4/4/4), Google Sheets (17: 12/4/1/0/0), Slack Web (174: 95/79/0/0/0); generated by Gemini 1.5 Pro from full OpenAPI specs then manually curated (9 samples fixed via automated-check failures, 58 samples fixed via full manual review)
quantitative_results: Correct implementations (t), full completion: 0% (Qwen2.5-Coder, Llama 3.1) up to 60% (GPT-4o); best open-source: Code Llama 70B 30%, then StarCoder2 26%
Correct implementations (t), argument completion: 8% (DeepSeek-Coder 7B) to 40% (Code Llama 70B, best open-source); GPT-4o best overall at 77%

Full completion, executable-only (e) metrics for non-zero models: correct URLs 32%-72%, illegal URLs 14%-39%, correct methods >80% for most models, argument precision 51%-75%, argument recall 33%-69%, argument value conditional accuracy 53%-84%
Executability of generated code was 94% or higher for all models except the three with near-zero full-completion correctness

Full-completion Table III per-model correct implementations (t): CodeT5+ 16B 0.06, StarCoder 15.5B 0.12, StarCoder2 15B 0.26, DeepSeek-Coder 6.7B 0.18, DeepSeek-Coder-V2 16B 0.21, Qwen2.5-Coder 14B 0.00, Code Llama 13B 0.08, Code Llama 70B 0.30, Llama 3.1 8B 0.00, GPT-4o 0.60

Argument-completion Table IV per-model correct implementations (t): CodeT5+ 16B 0.12, StarCoder 15.5B 0.27, StarCoder2 15B 0.25, DeepSeek-Coder 6.7B 0.26, DeepSeek-Coder-V2 16B 0.35, Qwen2.5-Coder 14B 0.37, Code Llama 13B 0.12, Code Llama 70B 0.40, Llama 3.1 8B 0.05, GPT-4o 0.77
baselines_compared: 21 open-source code LLMs: CodeT5+ (16B), StarCoder (15.5B), StarCoder2 (3B/7B/15B), DeepSeek-Coder (1.3B/6.7B/7B/33B), DeepSeek-Coder-V2 (16B), Qwen2.5-Coder (0.5B/1.5B/3B/7B/14B/32B), Code Llama (7B/13B/70B), Llama 3.1 (8B/70B)
GPT-4o and GPT-4o mini (OpenAI, commercial) used as an upper-bound reference, not open-source baselines
